title: What an Endocrinologist Should Know About the Surgical Treatment of Obesity

items.title: Why bariatric surgery is the most effective obesity treatment
items.text: Bariatric surgery produces 20–35% weight loss and dramatically improves weight-related comorbidities, especially type 2 diabetes. Evidence shows reduced cancer and cardiovascular mortality with modern low perioperative risk.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Bariatric surgery is the most effective treatment currently available for weight loss, and it dramatically improves weight-related comorbidities, especially type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM)."

items.title: How lifestyle, medications, and surgery compare for obesity
items.text: Lifestyle yields ~3–8% loss; antiobesity medications add ~3–10% beyond lifestyle; surgery provides 20–35% loss. Each shows wide interindividual variability.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Lifestyle treatment alone typically provides 3% to 8% weight loss depending on the intensity of treatment"

items.title: Current bariatric procedure distribution in the United States
items.text: Vertical sleeve gastrectomy accounts for ~61% of cases; RYGB ~17%; adjustable gastric banding is rarely performed; BPD/DS offers greatest weight loss but highest nutritional risk.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "VSG is now the most common operation performed, accounting for 61% of all bariatric surgeries."

items.title: BMI thresholds for bariatric surgery consideration
items.text: Consider surgery for BMI >40 kg/m², or BMI >35 kg/m² with weight-related comorbidity. Growing evidence supports benefit in T2DM with BMI 30–35 when glycemia remains uncontrolled despite aggressive medical therapy.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Bariatric surgery (adjustable gastric banding [AGB], vertical sleeve gastrectomy [VSG], Roux-en-Y gastric bypass [RYGB], and biliopancreatic diversion with duodenal switch [BPD/DS]) provides 20% to 35% weight loss and is appropriate to consider in patients with a BMI greater than 40 kg/m² or in those with a BMI greater than 35 kg/m² who also have a weight-related comorbidity"

items.title: How endocrinologists optimize patients before bariatric surgery
items.text: Screen vitamin deficiencies; optimize T2DM (HbA1c <8%), sleep apnea, and comorbidities; counsel on lifelong supplementation, avoiding NSAIDs/smoking, and pregnancy prevention for 12–18 months postoperatively.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Preoperative evaluation includes screening for preexisting vitamin deficiencies; optimizing medical conditions such as T2DM and sleep apnea"

items.title: Why modern bariatric surgery perioperative risk is acceptable
items.text: MBSAQIP-accredited laparoscopic programs report ~0.1% mortality—lower than laparoscopic cholecystectomy (0.7%) or hip replacement (0.93%). 30-day RYGB complication rates are comparable to common elective surgeries.
items.reference: Risks: "Currently, when performed by an accredited program, bariatric surgery has a lower mortality rate than that seen with laparoscopic cholecystectomy or hip replacement (0.1%, 0.7%, and 0.93%, respectively)."

items.title: How to adjust diabetes and BP medications immediately after surgery
items.text: Stop scheduled insulin and use correction doses; avoid sulfonylureas. Cut antihypertensives in half and stop diuretics to prevent hypovolemia. Use liquid/crushed meds—avoid extended-release formulations.
items.reference: Medication Management: "in the immediate postoperative period it is best to stop scheduled insulin and just use correction-factor insulin for high glucose levels."

items.title: How to recognize and treat post-bariatric thiamine deficiency
items.text: Wernicke encephalopathy presents 1–3 weeks postoperatively after protracted vomiting with ataxia, nystagmus, and confusion. Treat with parenteral thiamine 250–600 mg daily; prevention requires adequate thiamine beyond standard multivitamins.
items.reference: Thiamine Deficiency: "Treatment consists of parenteral thiamine administration of 250 to 600 mg daily depending on the severity of the symptoms."

items.title: How to monitor vitamin and mineral status after bariatric surgery
items.text: Monitor B₁₂, D, A, K, iron, copper, zinc, PTH, and bone health per procedure-specific schedules. B₁₂ deficiency may take >1 year; methylmalonic acid is more sensitive than serum B₁₂ alone.
items.reference: Vitamin B₁₂: "Simply testing serum vitamin B₁₂ levels may not be sufficiently sensitive to detect deficiency. Methylmalonic acid levels are a more sensitive measure of vitamin B₁₂ deficiency."

items.title: How to evaluate significant post-bariatric weight regain
items.text: Significant regain (~20% of lost weight) may signal mechanical surgical problems, lifestyle relapse, or medication effects. Upper GI series/endoscopy is indicated when abrupt appetite return follows years of stability—especially after band or dilated pouch.
items.reference: Weight Regain Following Weight-Loss Surgery: "The value that they determined was return of 20% of the lost weight."

items.title: Post-bariatric hypoglycemia diagnosis and first-line therapy
items.text: Symptomatic hypoglycemia 1–3 hours postprandially, typically 1–4 years after RYGB. Mixed-meal testing or CGM is preferred over OGTT; first-line therapy is frequent small low-GI mixed meals emphasizing protein.
items.reference: Post-Bariatric Surgery Hypoglycemia: "First-line therapy is frequent small mixed meals with low-glycemic index carbohydrates that emphasize protein."

items.title: Microvascular and macrovascular benefits of bariatric surgery in T2DM
items.text: Meta-analyses show ~74% reduction in microvascular complications vs medical therapy and ~48% reduction in macrovascular complications with ~79% lower 5-year mortality in large observational datasets.
items.reference: Microvascular and Macrovascular Disease: "bariatric surgery reduced the risk of microvascular complications by 74% compared with medical therapy."

items.question: Three weeks after RYGB, a man has progressive vomiting then confusion, ataxia, and abnormal eye movements. Most likely deficiency?
items.explanation: Acute Wernicke encephalopathy from thiamine deficiency is a medical emergency days to weeks postoperatively with vomiting.
items.reference: Case 1: "Answer: C) Thiamine"

items.question: For Wernicke encephalopathy after bariatric surgery, initial management includes:
options: Parenteral thiamine 200 mg TID for 3–5 days
items.explanation: Parenteral thiamine is urgent treatment; oral OTC multivitamins alone are insufficient acutely.
items.reference: Case 1: "the person should be treated with parenteral thiamine (200 mg 3 times daily for 3-5 days)."

items.question: A woman with prior AGB presents with weight regain, abdominal pain, nausea, and port-site erythema/tenderness. Most likely diagnosis?
items.explanation: Band erosion through the gastric wall with tracking along the port cannula causes port-site inflammation—classic late AGB complication.
items.reference: Case 2: "Answer: B) Band erosion"

items.question: Bariatric surgery is appropriate to consider for:
options: BMI >40 kg/m² or BMI >35 kg/m² with weight-related comorbidity
Any patient requesting cosmetic weight loss only
items.explanation: Standard thresholds are BMI >40 or >35 with comorbidity.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "appropriate to consider in patients with a BMI greater than 40 kg/m² or in those with a BMI greater than 35 kg/m² who also have a weight-related comorbidity"

items.question: Which bariatric procedure is now most commonly performed in the United States?
items.explanation: VSG accounts for ~61% of bariatric operations.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "VSG is now the most common operation performed, accounting for 61% of all bariatric surgeries."

items.question: Mortality for bariatric surgery in accredited laparoscopic programs is approximately:
items.explanation: Accredited bariatric surgery mortality ~0.1%, lower than cholecystectomy or hip replacement.
items.reference: Risks: "bariatric surgery has a lower mortality rate than that seen with laparoscopic cholecystectomy or hip replacement (0.1%, 0.7%, and 0.93%, respectively)."

items.question: Immediately after bariatric surgery in a patient with T2DM, insulin management should:
options: Stop scheduled insulin and use correction insulin for hyperglycemia
Start SGLT-2 inhibitor at full dose day 1
items.explanation: Glucose improves rapidly postoperatively; scheduled insulin risks hypoglycemia—use correction doses only initially.
items.reference: Medication Management: "it is best to stop scheduled insulin and just use correction-factor insulin for high glucose levels."

items.question: How long should pregnancy be avoided after bariatric surgery?
items.explanation: Pregnancy should be avoided for 12–18 months following the procedure.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Pregnancy should be avoided for 12 to 18 months following the procedure."

items.question: Which test is more sensitive than serum B₁₂ alone for detecting deficiency after RYGB/VSG?
items.explanation: Methylmalonic acid detects B₁₂ deficiency earlier than serum B₁₂.
items.reference: Vitamin B₁₂: "Methylmalonic acid levels are a more sensitive measure of vitamin B₁₂ deficiency."

items.question: Recommended daily vitamin D intake after bariatric surgery to maintain 25-OH vitamin D >30 ng/mL is:
items.explanation: All post-bariatric patients should consume 3000 IU vitamin D daily; some need much higher doses.
items.reference: Vitamin D: "All patients who have had bariatric surgery should consume 3000 IU of vitamin D daily to maintain 25-hydroxyvitamin D levels greater than 30 ng/mL"

items.question: Preferred calcium supplement after bariatric surgery is:
items.explanation: Calcium citrate is preferred because carbonate requires stomach acid for absorption.
items.reference: Calcium: "Because calcium carbonate is not well absorbed in the absence of stomach acid, calcium citrate is the preferred form for supplementation."

items.question: A study cited in the chapter defined clinically significant weight regain after bariatric surgery as:
items.explanation: Regain of 20% of lost weight was associated with comorbidity recurrence risk.
items.reference: Weight Regain Following Weight-Loss Surgery: "The value that they determined was return of 20% of the lost weight."

items.question: After bariatric surgery, hypertension remission at 1 year occurs in approximately what proportion of affected patients?
items.explanation: Remission occurs in 43–83% at 1 year, though recurrence can occur within 10 years.
items.reference: Hypertension: "surgery results in remission of hypertension in 43% to 83% of affected individuals at 1 year."

items.statement: Obesity is generally accepted by professional organizations as a disease of body weight regulation.
items.explanation: Obesity is a chronic disease, not simply a lifestyle choice.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "obesity is now generally accepted by virtually all professional organizations to be a disease of body weight regulation."

items.statement: Adjustable gastric banding is now rarely performed because efficacy is lower and complications higher than initially appreciated.
items.explanation: AGB use has declined markedly.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "AGB procedures are rarely performed any more, as efficacy is lower and complications are higher than had initially been appreciated."

items.statement: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs should be avoided after bariatric surgery because of anastomotic ulcer risk.
items.explanation: NSAIDs are contraindicated postoperatively.
items.reference: Management of Associated Health Conditions: "Those with degenerative joint disease should be transitioned off nonsteroidal antiinflammatory agents preoperatively, as these are contraindicated in the postoperative period."

items.statement: Anastomotic leak typically presents 3 to 4 days after surgery with tachycardia, fever, and shoulder pain.
items.explanation: Early postoperative leak is a surgical emergency with characteristic presentation.
items.reference: Anastomotic Leak: "This problem typically presents 3 to 4 days following surgery."

items.statement: Bariatric surgery is associated with reduced risk of obesity-associated cancers in large observational datasets.
items.explanation: Data from >630,000 patients suggest ~45% reduced risk of obesity-associated cancers.
items.reference: Cancer: "bariatric surgery is associated with a 28% reduced risk for developing all types of cancer, a 45% reduced risk of obesity-associated cancers"

items.statement: Six months of mandatory preoperative supervised weight loss improves long-term bariatric outcomes.
items.explanation: Insurance-required 6-month programs lack evidence for improving long-term outcomes.
items.reference: Nutritional Evaluation: "there is no evidence that this practice improves long-term outcomes."

items.statement: GLP-1 receptor agonists appear effective for weight loss in patients who have had bariatric surgery.
items.explanation: Studies suggest GLP-1 RAs work similarly post-surgery as in nonsurgical patients.
items.reference: Weight Regain Following Weight-Loss Surgery: "GLP-1 receptor agonist medications work just as well in patients following bariatric surgery as they do in nonsurgically treated patients."

items.statement: Bariatric surgery eliminates obstructive sleep apnea in all patients.
items.explanation: AHI improves substantially but many patients retain clinically significant apnea.
items.reference: Sleep Apnea: "While bariatric surgery can help many patients with sleep apnea, many are still left with clinically significant apnea."

items.assertion: Assertion: Bariatric surgery provides greater sustained weight loss than lifestyle or antiobesity medications alone.
items.reason: Reason: Lifestyle typically achieves 3–8% weight loss while surgery achieves 20–35%.
items.explanation: Both are true and quantitatively linked.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Bariatric surgery (adjustable gastric banding [AGB], vertical sleeve gastrectomy [VSG], Roux-en-Y gastric bypass [RYGB], and biliopancreatic diversion with duodenal switch [BPD/DS]) provides 20% to 35% weight loss"

items.assertion: Assertion: Wernicke encephalopathy after bariatric surgery requires urgent parenteral thiamine.
items.reason: Reason: Thiamine stores are depleted rapidly after prolonged postoperative vomiting.
items.explanation: Both are true; vomiting predisposes to acute thiamine deficiency.
items.reference: Thiamine Deficiency: "This typically presents 1 to 3 weeks following surgery in a patient who has several days of severe nausea and vomiting"

items.assertion: Assertion: Port-site erythema in a patient with AGB suggests possible band erosion.
items.reason: Reason: AGB has no mechanical components that can fail.
items.explanation: Assertion matches Case 2; bands are mechanical and can erode.
items.reference: Case 2: "Stomach contents then track up the inflation cannula causing skin redness in the area of the port."

items.assertion: Assertion: Vitamin B₁₂ deficiency usually appears within days after bariatric surgery.
items.reason: Reason: Body stores of B₁₂ are large and deficiency may take a year or more to develop.
items.explanation: Assertion is false; large stores delay deficiency—the reason is true.
items.reference: Case 1: "Vitamin B₁₂ deficiency (Answer A) can cause neurologic symptoms, but because of the large storage capacity of the body, deficiency usually does not appear until a year or 2 following surgery."

items.assertion: Assertion: HbA1c should ideally be less than 8.0% before elective bariatric surgery in patients with T2DM.
items.reason: Reason: Hyperglycemia has no bearing on surgical outcomes.
items.explanation: Assertion is true per chapter; ignoring glycemia is false.
items.reference: Management of Associated Health Conditions: "Ideally, hemoglobin A₁c levels should be less than 8.0% before surgery in those with T2DM."

items.assertion: Assertion: Long-term all-cause mortality is reduced after bariatric surgery compared with matched nonsurgical controls.
items.reason: Reason: Short-term perioperative mortality is slightly higher because of surgical risk.
items.explanation: Both are true: early perioperative mortality is slightly higher, but that does not explain long-term mortality reduction seen by 5–10 years.
items.reference: Overall Mortality: "by 5 to 10 years following surgery, several observational studies show reduced all-cause mortality in surgically treated patients as compared with matched patients who did not have surgery."

items.assertion: Assertion: Oral glucose tolerance testing is a poor diagnostic test for post-bariatric hypoglycemia.
items.reason: Reason: Many postoperative patients have abnormal OGTT without true post-bariatric hypoglycemia.
items.explanation: Both are true and causally linked.
items.reference: Post-Bariatric Surgery Hypoglycemia: "An oral glucose tolerance test is not a good diagnostic test, as many postsurgical patients will have abnormal values on this test and not have post-bariatric surgery hypoglycemia."

items.assertion: Assertion: Oral contraceptives may be less effective and thrombogenic after bariatric surgery.
items.reason: Reason: IUDs or other nonoral options may be preferable in the postoperative period.
items.explanation: Both are true; altered absorption and thrombosis risk support alternative contraception.
items.reference: Management of Associated Health Conditions: "Oral contraceptives may be less effective and may predispose to thromboembolic disease following bariatric surgery, so other options such as an intrauterine device might be considered."
